coredata.dc:title: Orthogonal experiments and bonding analysis of ultrasonic welded multi-layer battery foils and tabs
coredata.prism:publicationName: Engineering Science and Technology, an International Journal
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 THE AUTHORS. Published by Elsevier BV on behalf of Karabuk University.
coredata.prism:publisher: THE AUTHORS. Published by Elsevier BV on behalf of Karabuk University.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Lun
Su, Jianxiong
Li, Jiguang
Abbas, Zeshan
Dai, Haiping
Ma, Long
coredata.dc:description: The motivation for this research stems from the growing demand for solid-phase welding technology to join multilayers metals, particularly in green electric vehicles. Welding of foils and tabs inside battery is a challenging task due to poor joint formation at the interface and low strength. Ultrasonic welding is an efficient, reliable and environmentally friendly bonding method to firmly connect multi-layer copper foils and tabs. Therefore, this is used to achieve electrical bonding within the lithium-ion batteries. This is widely used in production of new energy vehicle batteries. In this work, 40 layers of copper foil were bonded to 0.3 mm nickel-plated copper tabs using ultrasonic metal welding technology. The effect of ultrasonic welding parameters on T-peel strength of joints was systematically investigated by orthogonal experimental design. The maximum T-peel strength of 359.17 N was obtained under optimal parameters (e.g., welding energy 600 J, pressure 0.3 MPa and amplitude 55 %). The variation of welding temperature and amplitude under different welding parameters was measured using infrared thermometer and laser vibrometer. Moreover, the surface, cross-sectional and failure fracture analysis and characterization of specimens with different weld qualities were performed using optical microscopy and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). In addition, the state of Ni layer in cross-section was analyzed using the energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) technique after ultrasonic welding. This approach can be straightforward and more appropriate to the development of solid-phase welding method for automotive industry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium ion battery
Ultrasonic welding
Orthogonal experiment
Multi-layer copper foil
Temperature analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental apparatus and material specification: (a) C20 ultrasonic welder (b) horn dimension drawing (c) Ni-plated Cu tab and (d) measurements of battery tab, all dimensions are in mm.
Main effect plot of mean value of T-peel load for Cu foil-tabs joint.
T-peel load mean main effect interaction plot.
Amplitude graph for different welding parameters.
Interface temperature rise for different welding parameters.
The surface moulding diagrams: (a-c) 5 groups (d-f) 23 groups and (g-i) 24 groups.
Stripping failure diagram: (a) 5 groups (b) 23 groups and (c) 24 groups.
(a) Different shapes of cross-sections (b) EDS scanning positions (c) EDS line scans.
Scanning analysis of EDS surface: (a) under-weld part (b) good-weld part and (c) over-weld part.
Orthogonal test welding parameters and levels.
Orthogonal table and tensile test results.
Mean response values of extreme deviation of the three factors.

head.ce:title: Orthogonal experiments and bonding analysis of ultrasonic welded multi-layer battery foils and tabs

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Ultrasonic Technology, Shenzhen Polytechnic University, Shenzhen 518055, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Ultrasonic Technology, Shenzhen Polytechnic University, Shenzhen 518055, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials and Metallurgy, University of Science and Technology Liaoning, Anshan 114051, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Materials and Metallurgy, University of Science and Technology Liaoning, Anshan 114051, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenzhen Institutes of Advanced Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shenzhen 518055, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Ultrasonics No.1 Intelligent Technology, Co., Ltd, Dongguan 523700, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Ultrasonics No.1 Intelligent Technology, Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Ultrasonics No.1 Intelligent Technology, Co., Ltd, Dongguan 523700, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute of Ultrasonic Technology, Shenzhen Polytechnic University, Shenzhen 518055, China..

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The motivation for this research stems from the growing demand for solid-phase welding technology to join multilayers metals, particularly in green electric vehicles. Welding of foils and tabs inside battery is a challenging task due to poor joint formation at the interface and low strength. Ultrasonic welding is an efficient, reliable and environmentally friendly bonding method to firmly connect multi-layer copper foils and tabs. Therefore, this is used to achieve electrical bonding within the lithium-ion batteries. This is widely used in production of new energy vehicle batteries. In this work, 40 layers of copper foil were bonded to 0.3 mm nickel-plated copper tabs using ultrasonic metal welding technology. The effect of ultrasonic welding parameters on T-peel strength of joints was systematically investigated by orthogonal experimental design. The maximum T-peel strength of 359.17 N was obtained under optimal parameters (e.g., welding energy 600 J, pressure 0.3 MPa and amplitude 55 %). The variation of welding temperature and amplitude under different welding parameters was measured using infrared thermometer and laser vibrometer. Moreover, the surface, cross-sectional and failure fracture analysis and characterization of specimens with different weld qualities were performed using optical microscopy and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). In addition, the state of Ni layer in cross-section was analyzed using the energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) technique after ultrasonic welding. This approach can be straightforward and more appropriate to the development of solid-phase welding method for automotive industry.

ce:section.ce:para: To reduce the dependence on oil and environmental pollution, the development of electric vehicles has been accelerated in many countries. Recently, the implementation of EVs in electric vehicle batteries is considered as a unsurpassed solution to the energy crisis and environmental issues [1]. The role of batteries is the most important as it is considered a main component and energy source of new energy vehicles [2]. Lithium-ion batteries are considered one of the most important inventions in the history of mankind and significantly improving the efficiency of energy use. These are widely used in the field of new energy vehicles [3]. Typically, a standard automotive battery pack consists of hundreds and even thousands of individual Li-ion batteries that are connected in series or parallel to achieve the required power and energy [4]. The welding has a high potential for joining individual battery cells or even multi-cell battery components. The stable electrical conductivity and mechanical strength are necessary to weld cells and modules.

Therefore, the welding techniques that can fulfil these requirements are necessary for joining foils and tabs [5]. Friction stir welding (FSW), laser welding and ultrasonic welding (USW) have been widely used for welding multi-layer foils recently. The FSW is advantageous to use as a flexible joining method for multi-layer copper sockets and has a short processing time, low cost and low welding temperature. However, it is also very susceptible to serious flash, notches, holes or other welding defects [6]. Laser welding is known for its speed and efficiency, localized energy and heat input and high degree of automation. But welding copper is challenging because the infrared laser radiation is reflected up to 95 % by a copper surface in the solid state leading to low process efficiency. It is also prone to defects such as porosity and rough weld surface [7,8]. The USW can weld high multilayer thin conductive materials and keep joint resistance low. The welding process does not generate large amounts of heat leading to melting of the metal, eliminating the metallurgical defects that are prevalent in most other fusion welds. Similarly, the USW is well suited for battery manufacturing and there is no universally established definition of weld quality [9–11].

The applicability of the USW process for battery connection in new energy vehicles has been investigated by various research groups such as Go et al. investigated the effect of process parameters on the formation of ultrasonically welded Al/Cu dissimilarity welds. The study found that a high welding time causes excessive deformation around the joint. It further results in reduction of tensile shear loads [12]. Zhang et al. used Cu-intercalated NiTi shape memory alloy for USW. The intense plastic deformation and high strain rate of the process led to recrystallisation of Cu grains and mutual diffusion of parent elements. Thus, a good metallurgical bond was formed between NiTi and Cu [13]. Jia et al. developed a finite element simulation model of ultrasonic welding. The aluminum foil simulation is performed and the model is optimized. The aluminum foil stress increases with the increase of welding pressure and the interface temperature decreases with the increase of aluminum foil thickness [14]. Satpathy et al. explored the modeling and optimization of USW for dissimilar thin plates based on fuzzy genetic algorithm. The most influencing parameter on the response is a vibration amplitude as it occupies rank 1 followed by weld time and weld pressure [15]. Shahid et al. soldered one layer of 100 µm nickel and two layers of 10 µm copper. The study established the optimum process parameters (Pressure = 0.2 MPa, Amplitude = 60 %, time = 0.3 s) to inaugurate the properties related to quality of the solder [16]. Nunes et al. proposed high surface roughness experimental results using Cu-Cu sheets. The study originated the rough surfaces using USW which is detrimental to mechanical interlocking and micro-welding. Nevertheless, there won't be enough friction between the sheets to form a connection at very low surface roughness. A straightforward acetone cleaning can result in higher tensile strength, when aluminum cables were used to link the USW joints [17]. Pöthig et al. used laser technology to treat the copper terminal surface, which improved the tensile strength [18]. Abbas et al. proposed a conformal approach to the surface of dissimilar metal sheets. The study was used to investigate the strength of welded joints using ultrasonic welding. Besides, the ultrasonically welded wire harness-terminal connections were investigated and the optimum stability was obtained after grooving the terminals. Moreover, its failure load, failure displacement and energy absorption value are increased by 15 %, 87 % and 113 %, respectively [19,20]. Zixin et al. investigated the effects of three different profiles on the mechanical properties and grain size of self-piercing riveting joints using ultrasonic welding. The application of ultrasound to the joints resulted in a 25.6 % increase in the average peak load, 31.1 % increase in the average breaking displacement and 88.8 % increase in the average energy absorption of the joints [21,22].

A number of factors influence the quality of ultrasonically welded joints; welding parameters are one of the most important. A systematic design of tests is typically required to optimize the welding settings, which is a crucial step in improving the quality of welded joints [23]. In quality engineering, the orthogonal technique is a popular and effective way to design experiments [24]. It uses an enhanced version of the usual design of experiments (DOE) method, which can efficiently ascertain the functional link between variables under control and anticipated results. More crucially, the orthogonal technique can drastically reduce the number of tests while maintaining experimental precision [25–27]. Therefore, a good idea is used to maximize the quality of ultrasonically welded connections. The orthogonal technique for experimental design in research or industrial practice is used to minimize the welding challenges. In short, multiple-layer copper foil welding has not received adequate attention in practical welding.

In this work, a nickel-plated copper electrode tabs and a multilayer of copper foil were welded together using USW process. Among other process parameters, the impacts of welding energy, amplitude and pressure were statistically examined using the orthogonal experimental approach regarding mechanical characteristics and welding temperature. In addition, SEM and EDS methods were also used to assess the cross-sectional forming quality and microstructural behavior under various welding settings.

The study utilized 40 layers of 99.99 % pure copper foil, measuring 6 µm in thickness and a nickel-plated copper tab of 50 mm in length, 50 mm in width and 0.3 mm in thickness. After USW, the copper tab is placed in the electric vehicle battery's positive electrode cell, serving as an electrode tab [28]. The welding apparatus is a C20 USW machine with an operating frequency of 20 kHz and a maximum output power of 3 kW as shown in Fig. 1 (a). The USW horn's dimensions are measured and displayed in Fig. 1(b). Numerous peaks and multiple welding locations are utilized for the copper foil and copper sheet to produce a robust connection. The horn's peaks are removed to avoid piercing the copper foil and inflicting welding damage. Fig. 1(c) shows the photograph of Ni-plated Cu tab. All the measurements of battery tab are shown in Fig. 1(d).

The complete ultrasonic metal welding process is shown in Fig. 2 : (1) copper foils and nickel-plated copper tab are positioned beneath the horn and enter the correct welding parameters; (2) the ultrasonic welding machine starts processing and the horn moves downwards under the pressure provided by the cylinder. The horn is used to apply a fixed clamping pressure before the start of welding; (3) the ultrasonic generator receives 60 Hz AC power from the power source which causes the transducer to produce mechanical vibrations in response to the ultrasonic signal. The booster amplifies the mechanical vibration which is then transferred to the weld material via the horn [29]. Therefore, under the action of the weld head of the ultrasonic welder, relative lateral movements are generated between the workpieces. In addition, the welding head and workpiece above of the position create friction with each other and shear plastic deformation occurs first at the micro-convexities on the surface of workpiece. The surface oxides are removed by the ultrasonic softening effect and the metal contact surfaces are exposed with many fresh contact points. The frictional heat generated by 20 kHz ultrasonic vibration energy which favours metallurgical bonding at the weld interface; (4) the horn vibration pauses and retains pressure for a while and then goes higher to complete the welding process.

A motorized tensile machine (SIC-500 KG, Japan) was used to perform the T-peel test by following sample preparation. This is used to measure the joint strength as shown in Fig. 3 . The specimen was bent 90 degrees in both directions at the front end of the welded area for peel test. The upper fixtures were held according to the foil and tabs in place by the bottom fixture. To analyze mechanical properties, the fixture was stretched at a rate of 50 mm/min during the tests conducted at room temperature. The microstructure of the cross-section was examined using optical microscopy, SEM and EDS after polishing with 2000 mesh SiC sandpaper.

The three levels this experiment such as weld energy, amplitude and pressure were adjusted as the study's primary factors as given in Table 1 . Additionally, it was determined that the L27 set of experiments should be designed using the orthogonal design of the experiments approach to account for the significant parameters and their interactions. The experimental design for the L27 series of tests is presented in Table 2 . Ten samples were welded for each set of experiments and seven of them were used for the peel test.

The analysis of extreme variance of the orthogonal test can aid in the interpretation of test results and provide valuable guidance for the test design and optimization process. In this paper, the extreme variance method is used in Minitab software to determine the optimal solution for each combination of factors. Hence, the calculation formula is as follows in Eq. (1):
(1)
R j
=
k
ij
( max )
-
k
ij
( min )
where, j denotes the number of factors and i denotes the number of levels. Similarly, kij is the mean value of the test tensile properties corresponding to the jth column of factors and the ith level and Rj is the extreme deviation of the jth column of factors.

In accordance with the above formulae, the extreme deviation of the three factors of welding energy, amplitude and pressure were calculated respectively. Moreover, the results are presented in Table 3 . As can be explored from the results, the order of factors affecting the peel strength of welded joints is: energy (73.12 %) > pressure (14.82 %) > amplitude (12.06 %).

In order to show more intuitively, the trend of the experimental evaluation indexes with the level of factors and the main effect plot is drawn as shown in Fig. 4 . The results investigated that when the welding parameters corresponding to the level of 3, 2, 2 applied the following values; e.g., welding energy 600 J, amplitude 55 %, pressure 0.3 MPa, then copper foil-tabs ultrasonic welded joints can obtain the maximum peeling load of 359.17 N which is the best welding parameters.

In the USW test process, it is difficult to distinguish the data fluctuations caused by welding parameters and test errors due to the extreme difference analysis. It is also not possible to quantitatively analyse the significance of the effect of each welding parameter on the tensile force test index [30,31]. Therefore, it is necessary to analyse the test results by ANOVA. The formula for ANOVA is as follows in Eqs. (2–6):
(2)
S
S T
=
Σ
i = 1
n
x i 2
-
1 n
(
Σ
i = 1
n
x i
)
2
(3)
S
S j
=
m n
Σ
i = 1
m
K
ij
2
-
1 n
(
Σ
i = 1
n
x i
)
2
(4)
S
S T
= S
S j
+ S
S e
(5)
MS =
SS
f
(6)
F j
=
M
S j
M
S e
where, SST is the total sum of squares of deviations and SSj is the sum of squares of deviations of factor j. Further, SSe is the square of deviations of errors and MS is the deviation. Likewise, i is the test number, xi is the test index, n is the total number of tests and m is the number of levels. Finally, Kij is the sum of the test indexes of factor j and level. In addition, i, fj is the degrees of freedom of factor j and Fj is the significance test statistic of factor j.

The significance level α = 0.05 was chosen to determine the level of significance of each influential factor. If, P>ɑ, the factor is considered to have no significant effect on the tested results. Conversely, it can be considered to have a significant effect. According to the results of ANOVA in Table 4 , it can be seen that there is a significant effect of energy on the mechanical properties of ultrasonic welded joints of copper foil-tabs. Moreover, the interaction between energy and amplitude is also more significant, the rest of the welding parameter effects are not significant. It can be judged that the degree of influence of each factor on the test results and the influence of the primary relationship are as follows: energy > energy × amplitude > pressure > amplitude × pressure > amplitude > energy × pressure. At the same time, in order to visualize the interactions between the factors, a T-peel load mean main effect interaction plot was drawn as shown in Fig. 5 . The reason for the significant effect of the interaction is explained by the amplitude analysis.

Amplitude plays an important role to investigate the analysis of fatigue strength assessment in welded joints. Table 4 mean ANOVA shows that only energy × amplitude has a significant effect under the interaction of welding parameters. Thus, this amplitude analysis does not consider the interaction of pressure and other parameters. The welding amplitude and its interaction were analysed by using a laser vibrometer to actually measure the actual horn vibration amplitude for each set of parameters. Fig. 6 shows the amplitude of vibration during welding with average vibration amplitudes of 16.84 μm, 22.61 μm and 27.9 μm at 40 %, 55 % and 70 % stability, respectively. Increasing the energy at a fixed weld pressure results in a significantly higher weld time and a significantly elongated amplitude graph; this at low energies is more in line with the high-energy front portion of the graph. Similarly, by increasing the amplitude at a fixed weld energy results in a shorter welding time and an increase in the amplitude of the vibration. It is worth noting that the amplitude is first increased in a peak, then down to certain amplitude and then up again, and then remains stable until the weld is complete. This phenomenon will be extremely obvious under the combined effect of high energy and amplitude as shown in Fig. 6 (Group 26). This is because the devices need a period of time to achieve a stable power output in the initial stages of USW. The load begins to follow the frequency and the amplitude gradually increases in the experiments. After reaching a certain level, some of the electrical energy is converted into heat resulting in an increase in temperature. So the thermal expansion and softening of the material results in a change in the horn contact impedance and this affects the output amplitude. Afterwards, a stable dynamic equilibrium is formed between the device and the material. Also, the amplitude remains relatively constant.

Fig. 6 shows the amplitude of 40 % is parallel to 55 % and 70 % amplitude at lower energy (200–400 J) conditions with no significant interaction. However, there is a significant interaction at higher energies (400–600 J). This is due to the amplitude of vibration is smaller at lower amplitudes. Equally, the welder applies welding energy to the material by increasing the welding time. Even at low energies, the welding time is long enough for the load to complete the ample frequency chasing process and for the amplitude to reach a steady state. The welding time is shortened under high amplitude conditions, resulting in the amplitude not being able to complete the full load frequency tracking process at lower energies. The complete “arc” does not appear in the amplitude graph and the amplitude is not in a steady state until the end of weld formation. However, the process of load frequency tracking can be completed at higher amplitude and high energy conditions. As a result, there was a significant change in the forming effect of the copper foil–tab welded joint. Whether, the load frequency tracking process of the horn can be completed or not depends on the dual effect of the input welding energy and the amplitude. Therefore, the interaction of welding energy and amplitude has a significant effect on the welded joint.

In order to investigate the presence of metal melting during USW of multilayer copper foils to pole tabs, this section demonstrates the temperature rise at the interface due to parameter changes. The surface temperature of the copper foil during welding was measured using an infrared thermometer. Likewise, the peak temperatures were recorded for each group. The temperature rise mainly occurs during ultrasonic vibration applied to the horn under static pressure. Furthermore, the friction between the copper foils is generated. The copper foils and the tabs are considered a main heat source for the temperature rise of welded joint.

The interfacial temperature rises with increasing energy, while pressure and vibration amplitude do not have a significant effect on the temperature as shown in Fig. 7 . The average temperature increases from 88.23 °C to 163.29 °C when the energy is increased from 200 J to 600 J. The temperature of the surface of the Cu foils increases with the increase in energy. However, the surface temperature of copper foil can reach up to 228.2 °C. The welding temperature is much lower than the melting point of copper, indicating that USW is a solid-state connection and does not involve melting of the metal. It was investigated that the peak temperature reaches the theoretical lower limit of the dynamic recrystallisation (DRX) temperature of copper, whether DRX occurs needs to be further analysed [32,33]. The sharp increase in interface temperature during USW significantly reduces the flow stresses in the material and makes the contact area more susceptible to deformation. Therefore, the copper atoms at the interface diffuse and interact to form metallic bonds when the temperature increase provides sufficient activation energy for diffusion of the atoms. Finally, it results in the metallurgical bonding of the joint [34].

The microscopic morphology observation can assist to analyze the mechanical properties of welded joints and provide a basis for investigating the mechanism of USW. The experimental samples of different welding parameters lead to the investigation of different shaping results. So, they are divided into three categories: under-welding, good-welding and over-welding. The surface morphologies of the welded groups are demonstrated in Fig. 8 . The surface morphology of the under-welded joints illustrates the surface of copper foil which appears smooth and flat with almost no cracks appearing as shown in Fig. 8(a). However, it can be observed in cross-section that most of the bonding surfaces of the copper foil have formed mechanical embedding under the effect of plastic deformation as shown in Fig. 8(b-c). This indicates that only part of the oxide layer on the surface of the copper foil was ruptured. Also, it can be observed from the cross-section that there is a significant small gap between the copper foil and the copper sheet. This is due to insufficient plastic flow of the material, which fails to completely fill the space between the two samples. It results the formation of stepped protrusions at the bonding surface. All these phenomena indicate that the welded joint failed to achieve the desired metallurgical bonding, resulting in a poor weld quality.

Fig. 8(d) shows the surface moulding of the good-weld group with only a few microcracks present on the surface of the copper foil. There is a collapse in the center of the horn teeth marks which is caused by the butt joint of the horn and anvil teeth marks lead to energy penetrates the copper foil during welding. It can be observed that the microcracks that appear on the surface of the copper sheet do not extend into the inside of the sheet as shown in Fig. 8(e-f). The peel force is at its maximum level and there is no gap in the cross section of the joint. This is due to ultrasonic softening and vibrational removal of surface oxides, and activation of the atoms between the joint interfaces in frictional warming. The electron cloud between the nuclei of the two atoms is dense and forms a bonded electron bridge when the static pressure brings the metal atoms close to each other down to the nanometre scale. The Mechanical embedding is thus transformed into interatomic bonding [35,36].

The excessive welding parameters directly resulted in over-welding of the joints and degradation of the moulding quality as shown in Fig. 8(g-i). The large cracks existed on the surface of the copper foil was due to the stress concentration in the horn caused by the higher pressure. Fatigue cracks are produced in the joint under the action of high frequency ultrasonic vibration [37]. Fatigue cracks produced in the joint have extended directions from the surface to the interior of the joint as shown in Fig. 8 (h-i). This part of the crack will be the weak location during the peeling experiment and the failure will be preferred by the crack. In addition, it can produce deep penetration of fatigue in the welded joint. It will reduce the mechanical properties of the welded joint. During the stripping experiments, over-welding of the samples resulted in fracture of the joints with different morphologies. This is further explained in the failure analysis.

The fracture micro-morphology of the welded joint is shown in Fig. 9 . It can be seen that when the access of welding parameter is low, the fracture occurs in the middle interface between the copper foil and pole tabs. Also, it will generate tear marks only at the welding point. This indicates that the friction generated by the ultrasonic vibration of the horn is lower, when the welding parameters are lower. The ultrasonic softening deformation is small and only mechanical interlocking is formed between some copper foils [38]. So, weld tears around the center of the weld joint reduce the effect when the welding parameters are optimal. The joint gradually forms a stable bond from the inside of the joint outwards, resulting in a high quality weld. The strength of the joint is higher than that of the copper foil. Therefore, it fails due to the low effect of parameters around the welded joint. The stripping failure diagram of the 5 groups is shown in Fig. 9(a). When the effect of welding parameters are too high, fatigue microcracks are produced in the welded joint due to high pressure and high-frequency ultrasonic vibration. The joint breaks along the microcrack extension and the layers around the welded joint are separated by tearing forces when subjected to peeling forces. The materials tear at each other and break to produce an incomplete welded joint. The effect of high parameters was investigated in sets 23 and 24 as shown in Fig. 9(b, c).

Due to the staggered arrangement of the teeth marks of a horn, there are multiple cases of the anvil's docking position with the horn, resulting in multiple shapes in the cross-sections as shown in Fig. 10 (a). However, the different docking proved to have no significant effect on the performance of T-peeling. Equally, the peel strength was always kept within a reasonable interval. Wherein, the different cross-sectional shapes resulted in large differences due to distribution of nickel layer on the surface of a tabs. Therefore elemental energy spectroscopy (EDS) analysis was required in order to determine the state of a nickel layer. The wider region of the cross-section is selected for EDS line scanning as shown in Fig. 10(b). We can observe that a stable transition phase is formed at the weld interface which consists of two elements, Ni and Cu as shown in Fig. 10(c). The horizontal coordinates of the EDS line scan plot investigated the position of elements, while the vertical coordinates represent the relative content of elements. there is a large fluctuation in the count rate of Cu elements before the line scan reaches the Ni layer. It is related to the flatness of a cross-section. The count rate of Cu elements decreases rapidly after encountering the Ni layer and the count rate of Ni elements increases rapidly from zero point. The two elements meet near 4500 cps(counts per second).

In order to further determine the connection characteristics at the interfaces of different groups of welder parameters, the movement and distribution of the nickel-plated layer on the surface of the electrode tabs were analyzed using EDS surface scanning maps. The nickel-plated layer of the electrode tab of the under-welded group has an obvious continuous interface in the lower part of the weld as shown in Fig. 11 (a). There are only a few areas of faults in the analysis. It indicates that the friction between the foil layer and the pole ear is not sufficient. Therefore, there are few metallurgical bonding areas where the nickel layer is broken. The Ni layer obviously appears more broken areas and part of the nickel layer is overlapped with the nearby nickel layer as shown in Fig. 11(b). This is due to the deformation of already metallurgically bonded points under the action of ultrasonic vibration and formed by repeated folding and squeezing [39]. Moreover, some of the continuous nickel layers indicate that the bonding at the weld interface is heterogeneous. However, the nickel layer becomes thinner in the over-welded region as shown in Fig. 11(c). This is due to the higher welding parameters accelerating the elemental intercalation between Cu and Ni and the higher welding pressure inhibits the folding deformation of the nickel layer. There is no IMC between Cu and Ni during USW and the micro bonding is just diffusion of atoms [40,41]. Also, the plated nickel layer is only 1 μm thick. So, the diffused nickel is no longer obvious in the EDS. The main purpose of nickel plating is to prevent corrosion, but nickel has a higher resistance than copper [42]. Likewise, the high electrical conductivity is required for battery application joints because of the need to transmit electrical energy. Therefore, when the nickel layer is discontinuous at the interface, the loss of electrical energy in the joint can be reduced.

In this work, the effect of process parameters on the peeling strength of 40 layer copper foils and tabs was investigated based on the orthogonal test. The optimum process parameters were achieved. The temperature rise of the ultrasonically welded joints was measured. The joint formation and t-peel fracture morphology were observed and investigated. The conclusions are summarized as follows.
1)
Welding energy has a positive effect on peel strength of the joints, which increases and then decreases with the increase in welding pressure and amplitude. Under the optimum combination of parameters (e.g., energy = 600 J, pressure = 0.3 MPa, amplitude = 55 %), the maximum T-peel strength reached 359.17 N. The order of the factors affecting the tensile load of the welded joints was as follows: energy (73.12 %) > pressure (14.82 %) > amplitude (12.06 %).
2)
Analysis of variance (ANOVA) shows that both welding energy and welding energy × amplitude interactions have a significant effect on the mechanical properties of ultrasonically welded joints of copper foil-tabs. Likewise, the effective mechanism of interaction of the welding parameters is analyzed by the amplitude diagram.
3)
The temperature rise at the interface increases with the increase in welding energy, whereas pressure and vibration amplitude have almost no significant effect on the temperature rise. At energy of 600 J, the interface temperature reaches a maximum of 228.2 °C. The surface temperature is well below the melting point of copper, indicating that the USW of copper foils is a solid state connection.
4)
Under-welding was investigated by SEM analysis at lower welding parameter samples with smooth surfaces but voids in the cross-section. Failure residue is low and there is a lot of mechanical interlocking in the bonding pattern. At the optimum welding parameters, the weld was superior with a fewer cracks on the surface but a high quality of cross-section formation. Failure occurs around the weld joint and there is a lot of interatomic bonding. Sample over-welded at higher parameters with fatigue micro-cracks on the surface were found. In the cross-section, with failure preferentially were occurred from the micro-cracks.
5)
In the EDS surface scan, it is found that the nickel layer is obvious and continuous in the under-welded group and solitary a small part is broken. The nickel layer in the over-soldered group is significantly more fractured and folded. The diffusion of the nickel layer into the copper is no longer evident in the over-soldered group.

Lun Zhao: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Jianxiong Su: Visualization, Validation, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jiguang Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Formal analysis. Zeshan Abbas: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Haiping Dai: Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Long Ma: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by Scientific Research Startup Fund for Shenzhen High-Caliber Personnel of SZPT (No.6022310046K; No.6024330003K); National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12104324); Post-doctoral Later-stage Foundation Project of Shenzhen Polytechnic（No. 6023271014K; No.6021211014K); Postdoctoral Startup Fund of Shenzhen Polytechnic University (No. 6022331008 K).
